elements.text: submit to the lender a title policy and assist the lender in obtaining the appraisal and a survey;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: agree that all renovation work will be completed no later than 15 months from the date the loan is closed and in accordance with the terms of the renovation contract (subject to approved change orders);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: permit the lender to make property inspections; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: pay all costs and expenses required to satisfy any conditions of the agreement (including costs overruns, the costs of change orders, and the costs of enforcement of the agreement in the event of default);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: state the terms and conditions under which the lender may extend the 15 month completion deadline if the renovation cannot be completed on time (due to extenuating circumstances outside of the borrower's control), including
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: limiting any extension to no later than 18 months after the loan closing; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: requiring any extension to be in writing and the borrower to not be in default under any of the terms of the loan documents or the renovation contract;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include provisions related to
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the time, manner, and method by which the lender disburses the loan proceeds;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: conditions on how the disbursements may be used;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: procedures on how to request a disbursement (including the proper format, information, and required signatories);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: documentation required to support each request for disbursement (such as the title policy, any required lien waivers from all contractors, subcontractors, and suppliers) and any required inspection reports;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the number and amount of payments that the lender is to make to the borrower and/or the contractor; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: obligate the borrower and the contractor to enter into a renovation contract for all labor and materials to renovate the improvements, and provide the lender with a copy of
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: that contract;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the applicable plans and specifications that fully describe all work to be performed;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the renovation budget (which provides a timetable for stages of completion and the schedule for disbursements for payment of amounts due);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a schedule of disbursements for payment of the renovation costs; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the requirements for requesting (and obtaining approval of) change orders.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae has developed a model Renovation Loan Agreement (Form 3731) to document the renovation loan agreement between the borrower and the lender.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Because HomeStyle Renovation mortgages may be subject to a variety of laws and regulations based on the type of transaction or the type of lender originating the mortgages, a lender that uses Fannie Mae's model documents must make all appropriate, required changes to them.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The fully executed renovation loan agreement is a custodial document. The lender must maintain a copy of the agreement in the loan file and send the original to the document custodian as part of the loan delivery package
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: (see E-2-01, Required Custodial Documents, for additional information).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lien Waiver
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Before a lender makes the final disbursement upon the completion of renovation, it must obtain a lien waiver from the contractor, all subcontractors, and suppliers or a clear title report that releases all contractor, subcontractor, and supplier liens. Fannie Mae's model documentLien Waiver (Form 3739)-may be used for this, provided the lender makes any changes to it that are required by applicable law.

elements.text: B5-3.3-01, HomeStyle Refresh for Improvements on Existing Properties (12/10/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information concerning mortgage loans on existing properties where renovation improvements are part of the transaction, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Product Eligibility
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligible Property and Occupancy Types
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligible Improvements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Calculating LTV and Maximum Renovation Amount
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Responsibilities
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Appraisal Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Title and Insurance Updates
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Special Feature Code
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Manual Underwriting
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: HomeStyle Refresh provides borrowers with a financing option for smaller-scale home improvements, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: energy- or water-efficiency upgrades,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: environmental remediation,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: disaster preparedness or resiliency improvement, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: repairs needed due to a natural or environmental disaster of an existing property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A lender does not need special approval to deliver HomeStyle Refresh loans to Fannie Mae. These loans are delivered with recourse, which may be removed once the lender provides proof of completion and the loan remains current.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A lender may deliver a HomeStyle Refresh loan with eligible improvements as soon as the loan is closed. The eligible improvements do not have to be completed when the mortgage is delivered to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Product Eligibility
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Improvements are permitted on existing properties in conjunction with all standard Guide products and features including, but not limited to:
elements.label: text

elements.text: high-balance loans,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Community Seconds,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: loans with deed restrictions (including programs that allow below market rate mortgages),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: down payment assistance programs,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: HomeReady loans, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Community Land Trusts.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: HomeStyle Refresh loans are subject to the applicable LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios for purchase and limited cash-out refinance transactions found in the Eligibility Matrix . Improvements cannot be financed in the loan amount of a high LTV refinance loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Improvements are permitted on a cash-out refinance, however the transaction is not considered a HomeStyle Refresh loan. All standard cash-out refinance policies apply.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligible Property and Occupancy Types
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All one- to four-unit existing properties are eligible for HomeStyle Refresh. All property types are eligible. Manufactured homes are eligible provided the improvements do not include structural changes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: All occupancy types are permitted.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Eligible Improvements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The HomeStyle Refresh program may be used to make small-scale improvements and renovations to an existing property, such as:
elements.label: text

elements.text: kitchen or bathroom updates;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: construction of outdoor buildings and structures when allowed by local zoning regulations, for example swimming pools, decking, screening and porch, and patio additions;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: adding or renovating an accessory dwelling unit;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: improvements or repairs related to disaster damage or improvements to protect the property from future disaster, such as
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: storm surge barriers,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: foundation retrofitting for earthquakes,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: hazardous brush and tree removal in fire zones, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: retaining walls to address mud or water flows.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: environmental hazard damage repairs or resiliency improvements, including asbestos, lead, mold, and radon; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: energy- or water-efficiency improvements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Change Requests
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Before approving any change request a borrower wants to make to the original plans and specifications, the lender must require the borrower to submit a HomeStyle Change Order Request (Form 1200) or a substantially similar document, that provides a detailed description of the
elements.label: text

elements.text: change(s),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: cost of the change(s), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: estimated completion date(s).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Calculating LTV and Maximum Renovation Amount
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Purchase Transactions: In a purchase transaction, the proceeds can be used to finance the acquisition of the property and the cost of the improvements or the amount to payoff PACE debt. The LTV ratio is determined by dividing the original loan amount by the lesser of
elements.label: text

elements.text: the 'as completed' appraised value of the property
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the sum of the purchase price of the property plus the cost of the improvements, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the sum of the purchase price plus the total amount of PACE debt to be paid off.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Limited Cash-out Refinance Transactions: When a loan is originated as a limited cash-out refinance, the loan must meet all of the standard requirements for limited cash-out refinances (as described in B2-1.3-02, Limited Cash-Out Refinance Transactions).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Proceeds may also be used to pay off an existing PACE loan or other debt (secured or unsecured) that financed energy-related improvements. The standard cash back allowance of the greater of 1% of the loan amount or $2,000 is permitted on these loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For limited cash-out refinance transactions, the LTV ratio is determined by dividing the original loan amount by the 'as completed' appraised value of the property when the mortgage is being delivered prior to the completion of the improvements. If the appraisal was completed after the completion of the improvements, then the LTV ratio is determined by dividing the original loan amount by the appraised value of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: There is no minimum dollar amount for the improvements; the maximum dollar amount depends on the type of HomeStyle Refresh activity and the transaction, described in the table below
elements.label: text

elements.text: HomeStyle Refresh
elements.label: text

elements.text: Activity
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: property
elements.label: text

elements.text: For purchases or limited cash-out refinances up to 15% of the 'as
elements.label: text

elements.text: completed' appraised value of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Note:

elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Responsibilities
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender is responsible for
elements.label: text

elements.text: ensuring that the appraiser has been provided with a copy of all plans and specifications proposed for the renovation project,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: managing the completion escrow account in which improvement funds are held, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: monitoring the completion of the HomeStyle Refresh improvement work.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must establish a completion escrow for incomplete improvements. The improvements must be completed no later than 180 days from the date of the mortgage note. For requirements related to the completion of the postponed improvements, including escrow accounts, disposition of funds after work completion, and title reports, see the applicable table in B4-1.2-05, Requirements for Verifying Completion and Postponed Improvements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In the event a renovation project exceeds 180 days, the lender must submit this information to Loan Quality Connect to describe the circumstances resulting in the delay and determine potential remedies. Recourse shall remain until proof of completion has been provided. If a loan experiences a serious delinquency (120+ days) while under recourse, the loan will be subject to repurchase.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender may fund up to 50% of the total planned renovation costs at closing with an initial draw. A portion of the initial draw may be used to pay for permits, architect fees, and design or planning expenses that were incurred during the initial part of the project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The lender must maintain a copy of all documentation in the individual loan file that supports the improvement work, such as the 'as completed' appraisal, home improvement contract, certification of completion, and title insurance endorsements or updates (if applicable).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Lenders may not transfer servicing on HomeStyle Refresh loans during the renovation period.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Appraisal Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All HomeStyle Refresh loans require an appraisal based on an interior and exterior property inspection and must be completed on the appropriate form depending on the property type. When the loan is being delivered prior to the completion of the improvements, appraisers must determine the 'as completed' value of the property subject to the improvements being completed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Following completion of the renovation work, the lender must obtain a certification of completion stating the renovation was completed in accordance with the submitted plans and specifications. The certification must be documented on the Appraisal Update and/or Completion Report (Form 1004D). The 1004D must confirm all "subject to" items listed on the appraisal were completed. If deviations have been made to the initial plans, the appraiser must note any impact to the appraised value. A copy of the Completion Report must be submitted through Loan Quality Connect to confirm completion.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Title and Insurance Updates
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must obtain a title update with the renovation completion date before issuing the final funds to ensure that Fannie Mae's first lien priority remains in place and confirms that no mechanic's or materialmen's liens exist.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the property is located in a state in which contractors', subcontractors', or materialmen's liens have priority over mortgage liens, the lender must obtain all necessary lien releases or take any other actions that may be required to ensure title to the property is clear of all liens and encumbrances.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must also retain in the loan file a certification regarding the adequacy of the property insurance following completion of the renovation. The certification must confirm the coverage has been increased, if necessary, to comply with Fannie Mae's standard property and flood insurance requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Special Feature Code
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must report Special Feature Code 892 when delivering a loan that meets the HomeStyle Refresh requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Manual Underwriting
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For loans involving improvements that are underwritten manually, a maximum debt-to-income ratio of 45% is allowed if the transaction satisfies all criteria in the Eligibility Matrix for a 45% DTI ratio on a manually underwritten loan. All other underwriting requirements, such as the down payment, credit score, and reserve requirements, are identical to those for a similar transaction with a maximum DTI ratio of 36%.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .
elements.label: text

elements.text: Section B5-3.4, Property Assessed Clean Energy Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: B5-3.4-01, Property Assessed Clean Energy Loans (10/08/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on Property Assessed Clean Energy (PACE) loans, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligibility
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Refinancing Options for Properties with a PACE Loan
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Delivery Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Certain energy retrofit lending programs, often referred to as Property Assessed Clean Energy (PACE) programs, are made by localities to finance residential energy-related improvements and are generally repaid through the homeowner's real estate tax bill. These loans typically have automatic first lien priority over previously recorded mortgages. The terms of the Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac Uniform Security Instruments prohibit loans that have senior lien status to a mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae will not purchase mortgage loans secured by properties with an outstanding PACE loan unless the terms of the PACE loan program do not provide for lien priority over first mortgage liens. Lenders must monitor state and local law to determine which jurisdictions offer PACE loans that may provide for lien priority.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the PACE loan is structured as a subordinate lien or unsecured loan, the first mortgage loan may be underwritten to Fannie Mae's standard guidelines.
elements.label: text

elements.text: However, for PACE loans originated prior to July 6, 2010, Fannie Mae waives the uniform security instrument prohibition against a PACE loan with lien priority if the corresponding mortgage loan was purchased before July 6, 2010 or is in an MBS pool with an issue date on or before July 1, 2010.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: On the Form 1003 (1/2021) the borrower must indicate if the property will be subject to a PACE lien that will take priority over the first mortgage lien in Section 5a E. The lender must indicate if the property is currently subject to a a PACE lien that will take priority over the first mortgage lien in Section L1.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Refinancing Options for Properties with a PACE Loan
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following requirements apply to borrowers with loans that are owned or securitized by Fannie Mae who seek to refinance and who obtained a PACE loan prior to July 6, 2010:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Paying off the PACE loan: The lender must first attempt to qualify the borrower for either a cash-out or limited cash-out refinance option, with the PACE loan being paid off as part of the refinance. To mitigate the risk posed by PACE obligations that take lien priority over the mortgage, Fannie Mae requires that borrowers with sufficient equity pay off the existing PACE obligation as a condition to obtaining a new mortgage loan. The prohibition against using the proceeds of a limited cash-out refinance to pay off a loan not used to purchase the property will not apply.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loan casefiles underwritten in DU as a limited cash-out refinance may receive an Ineligible recommendation when it appears the borrower is receiving more than 1%/$2,000 cash back due to the payoff of a PACE loan. The lender may deliver the loan with the Ineligible recommendation and retain the DU limited waiver of underwriting representations and warranties provided that the mortgage loan meets the requirements of this Guide, including (but not limited to) A2-2-04, Limited Waiver and Enforcement Relief of Representations and Warranties
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Retaining the PACE loan: If the borrower is unable to qualify for a cash-out or limited cash-out refinance with sufficient proceeds to pay off the PACE loan, the lender may underwrite the loan as a limited cash-out refinance, with the PACE loan remaining in place. In these cases, it will not be necessary to include the PACE loan in the calculation of the CLTV ratio, though it must be included in the monthly housing expense (PITIA) and debt-to-income calculation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Delivery Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For those eligible limited cash-out refinances where the PACE loan remains in place, the loans must be delivered with SFC 173.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Section B5-4.1, General Requirements of Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: B5-4.1-01, Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loans (12/19/2017)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Eligibility
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loan Origination and Compliance
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Certification
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender's and Servicer's Obligations to Maintain Procedures for Curing Violations
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan is a loan originated in accordance with and secured by a lien permitted under the provisions of Article XVI, Section 50(a)(6), of the Texas Constitution, which allow a borrower to take equity out of a homestead property under certain conditions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Eligibility
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Unless otherwise notified in writing, all lenders are eligible to sell and/or service Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans as long as the lender meets the eligibility criteria specified in Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6). A lender that intends to sell Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans originated by a third-party originator is also responsible for ensuring that the originating lender qualifies as an 'authorized lender' under Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loan Origination and Compliance
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: In addition to Fannie Mae's other origination and compliance requirements for Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans in this chapter, lender agrees to the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Requirement
elements.label: text

elements.text: The borrower's first payment must be due no later than two months after closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For purposes of the compliance with the acknowledgment of the "fair market value" of the
elements.label: text

elements.text: homestead property requirement, the "fair market value" must be based on an appraisal and
elements.label: text

elements.text: the appraisal must be attached to the written acknowledgment. See B5-4.1-03, Texas Section
elements.label: text

elements.text: 50(a)(6) Loan Underwriting, Collateral, and Closing Considerations for Fannie Mae's appraisal
elements.label: text

elements.text: requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The proceeds from a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan must not be used to acquire or improve the
elements.label: text

elements.text: homestead if a loan for that purpose could have been made under a different provision of the
elements.label: text

elements.text: Texas Constitution.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae has no other restrictions on the use of the loan proceeds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the new loan is a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan refinance transaction originated to cure a failure in the original loan to comply with Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6), then the new loan is eligible for sale to Fannie Mae provided that it complies in all respects with Fannie Mae's requirements. However, unless a refinance transaction has been completed to cure a failure in the original loan transaction to comply with Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6), a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan is ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae if the lender has either identified or been notified by the borrower of a failure to comply, whether or not there has already been a cure or an attempt to cure the failure to comply.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU does not contain the specific eligibility rules needed to determine eligibility of Texas
elements.label: text

elements.text: Section 50(a)(6) loans under Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) or the
elements.label: text

elements.text: Selling Guide
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders must determine whether refinance loans secured by properties in Texas are eligible
elements.label: text

elements.text: for sale to Fannie Mae, and should be aware that even though a loan may receive an 'Eligible'
elements.label: text

elements.text: recommendation, the loan may not comply with Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) or be
elements.label: text

elements.text: eligible for delivery according to Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) or the
elements.label: text

elements.text: Selling Guide
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Certification
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: By sale of a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan to Fannie Mae, the lender represents, warrants, and certifies that with respect to all of the Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans delivered to Fannie Mae, whether or not originated by the lender:
elements.label: text

elements.text: All Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans were originated pursuant to written processes and procedures that comply with the provisions of the Texas Constitution applicable to mortgage loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender has in place a specific process for the receipt, handling, and monitoring of notices from
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: borrowers that lender (or the mortgage originator, if lender is the servicer but not the originator) failed to comply with the provisions of the law applicable to Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans. Such process must be adequate to ensure that the lender will correct the failure to comply by one of the authorized means no later than the 60th day after the date the lender is notified of the failure to comply by the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: An attorney familiar with the provisions of Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) was consulted in connection with the development and implementation of the processes and procedures used for the origination of the Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: To ensure ongoing compliance with the law applicable to loans authorized by Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6), the processes and procedures used for the origination of the Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans will be reviewed by the lender regularly and will be updated and revised, as appropriate pursuant to clarifications of the law, on a regular and continual basis.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender's and Servicer's Obligations to Maintain Procedures for Curing Violations
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders and servicers must have specific processes in place to cure any failure to comply with Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) identified with respect to a loan sold to or serviced on behalf of Fannie Mae by one of the authorized means, as required by the 'Lender Certification' requirements described above. A lender's or servicer's failure to cure within 60 days after being notified of a failure to comply may, under Texas law, result in the forfeiture of all principal and interest due under the Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan. However, any action taken, or not taken, in connection with a failure to comply with Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6), even if such action is a result of the lender's or servicer's effort to cure a failure to comply, that results in any of the following constitutes a breach of the lender's selling representations and warranties and/or servicing obligations and requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a forfeiture of any principal or interest due under the mortgage loan;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: invalidation of the mortgage as a first lien;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: abatement of accrual of interest and the borrower's obligations under the mortgage loan;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: reduction in the principal amount of the mortgage loan; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any modification of the amount, interest rate, term, or other provision of the mortgage loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Such action, taken or not taken, shall be deemed a failure to correct a significant defect and/or a servicing defect that permits Fannie Mae to exercise any of the remedies provided in the Lender Contract, including the right to require repurchase of the loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the lender or servicer receives notice from a borrower that a lender (or the mortgage originator, if the lender or the servicer is not the originator) failed to comply with Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6), the lender or servicer must immediately, but no later than seven business days after receipt, take the following actions:
elements.label: text

elements.text: inform Fannie Mae's Legal department by submitting a Non-Routine Litigation (Form 20) and include the borrower notice in its submission; and,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: collaborate with Fannie Mae on the appropriate response, including any cure that may be necessary, within the 60-day-time frame provided by the requirements of Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B5-4.1-02, Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loan Eligibility (12/16/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan eligibility, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refinance Classifications
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligible Loan Products and Transaction Types
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loan Security Property
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Refinance Classifications
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders should be aware that Fannie Mae's classification of loan transactions as 'cash-out refinance' or 'limited cash-out refinance' may differ from the way loans are classified under Texas law.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders should not rely on Fannie Mae's categorization of refinance loans for purposes of determining whether compliance with the provisions of Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) is required. Rather, such lenders should consult with their counsel to determine the applicability of Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) to a particular loan transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Texas law determines whether or not a loan is a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan, and Fannie Mae's policy determines whether the loan must be delivered as a cash-out refinance transaction or as a limited cash-out refinance transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender is responsible for determining:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the applicability of Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6) regardless of Fannie Mae's definitions of cash-out and limited cash-out refinance transactions; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if the loan should be delivered to Fannie Mae as a cash-out refinance or a limited cash-out refinance transaction, including the applicable special feature codes and payment of all applicable LLPAs.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: All loans that constitute Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans under Texas law must comply with these provisions, regardless of whether the loan is classified as a 'cash-out refinance' or 'limited cash-out refinance' in the Selling Guide . See B5-4.1-03, Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loan Underwriting, Collateral, and Closing Considerations
elements.label: text

elements.text: For any refinance of a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan that results in a loan originated in accordance with and secured by a lien permitted by Article XVI, Section 50(a)(4) of the Texas Constitution, an affidavit referenced in Section 50(f-1) Article XVI of the Texas Constitution must be prepared and recorded in connection with each such transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligible Loan Products and Transaction Types
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans must be fully amortizing loans with payments due on a monthly basis. The following are eligible as Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: first lien mortgages only;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: fixed-rate loans; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: five-, seven-, and ten-year ARM plans (4927, 4928, and 4929 Texas 50(a)(6), respectively).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: These ARM plans should be structured in the same way that they are for other loans, except that the loan may not be assumable at any time over its full term. Only the ARM plans listed above are eligible, due to the MBS disclosure impact resulting from the non-assumable nature of these ARMs.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following are not eligible as Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans:
elements.label: text

elements.text: loans that are not in first-lien position,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ARM plans not listed above, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: loans with temporary interest rate buydowns.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loan Security Property
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan must be secured by a single-unit principal residence constituting the borrower's homestead under Texas law. Loans secured by two- to four-unit properties, investment properties, or second homes are not eligible. The security property may be
elements.label: text

elements.text: a detached dwelling,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an attached dwelling,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a unit in a PUD project,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a unit in a condo project, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a manufactured home. (A manufactured home is eligible only if it is classified as real property under Texas law, and satisfies all special Fannie Mae eligibility criteria for manufactured homes.)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The borrower's homestead property may not exceed the applicable acreage limit as determined by Texas law when the Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan is originated.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A borrower that owns adjacent land must submit appropriate evidence, such as a survey, that the mortgaged homestead property is a separate parcel that does not exceed the permissible acreage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : An inter vivos revocable trust that meets Fannie Mae's borrower eligibility criteria (as described in B2-2-05, Inter Vivos Revocable Trusts), may be a borrower under a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan, provided that the trust meets the requirements for a "qualifying trust" under Texas law for purposes of owning residential property that qualifies for the homestead exemption.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B5-4.1-03, Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loan Underwriting, Collateral, and Closing Considerations (03/04/2026)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan underwriting, collateral, and closing considerations, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: LTV/CLTV Ratios
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Underwriting
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Property Valuation and Survey
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loan Documentation
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Title Insurance
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: LTV/CLTV Ratios
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Per Texas law, the maximum allowable LTV and combined LTV for any Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan is 80%, notwithstanding any conflicting provisions of this Guide or any specific DU recommendation or finding. HELOC subordinate financing is not permitted, hence a maximum HCLTV ratio is not applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Underwriting
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans are eligible for the reduced documentation requirements recommended by DU, provided that all other terms and conditions described herein for Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans shall apply.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan that represents the refinance of a prior Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan, the borrower must requalify even if the lender is currently servicing the existing loan that is being refinanced.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Manually underwritten Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans are subject to minimum credit score requirements per the Selling Guide , based on the transaction as either a cash-out refinance or a limited cash-out refinance, as applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Property Valuation and Survey
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must obtain a new appraisal to determine the property's current value even if DU offers value acceptance, or a value acceptance + property data option. If the appraisal is deemed deficient, refer to Guidance on Addressing Appraisal Deficiencies in B4-1.3-12, Appraisal Quality Matters. The appraisal (together with any applicable desk review or field review) must be attached to the written acknowledgment of "fair market value".
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: When a desk review or field review is requested by the lender in connection with a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan, it must be obtained prior to the date of the note and mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The appraisal for the property and the acknowledgment of fair market value must not include any property other than the homestead.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The survey (or other acceptable evidence) must demonstrate that:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the homestead property and any adjacent land are separate parcels, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the homestead property is a separately platted and subdivided lot for which full ingress and egress is available.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender selling the loan to Fannie Mae must not have any interest (such as an option to purchase, a security interest, or an easement) in any parcel adjacent to the homestead property that is owned by the borrower, if such interest could constitute additional security for the Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loan Documentation
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: There is a special security instrument, notes, and riders that must be used in connection with Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans and a special affidavit that must be prepared and recorded in connection with each Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan transaction. Lenders must use the following documents:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Texas Home Equity Security Instrument (First Lien) (Form 3044.1)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the specific Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan notes and riders, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Texas Home Equity Affidavit and Agreement (First Lien ) (Form 3185)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Because of the complexities involved in closing Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans, lenders must provide the title company with a detailed closing instruction letter and require an acknowledgment of its receipt.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The closing instructions must require the title company to conduct its closings properly to ensure compliance with Texas Constitution Section 50(a)(6). To assist in this endeavor, the Texas Home Equity Affidavit and Agreement First Lien (Form 3185) must be prepared and recorded in connection with each Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae suggests that a lender also require each borrower to sign a closing receipt that itemizes the documents that they received at closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For additional Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan documentation (also called 'Texas Home Equity' documentation) refer to Standard Texas Home Equity Notes (under Standard Instruments).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans are not eligible for delivery as eMortgages.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Title Insurance
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For all Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans, a title insurance policy written on Texas Land Title Association forms (standard or short form), supplemented by an Equity Loan Mortgage Endorsement (Form T-42) and a Supplemental Coverage Equity Loan Mortgage Endorsement (Form T-42.1), is required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : There may be no exceptions or deletions to the coverage provided by Paragraphs 2(a) through (e) (other than an exception or deletion relating to the exclusion of agricultural homestead property) of the T-42 endorsement. The endorsement must include the optional coverage provided by Paragraph 2(f), as well as the additional coverage provided by Endorsement T-42.1.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The title insurance policy cannot include language that:
elements.label: text

elements.text: excludes coverage for a title defect that arises because financed origination expenses are held not to be 'reasonable costs necessary to refinance', or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: defines the 'reasonable costs necessary to refinance' requirement as a 'consumer credit protection' law since the standard title policy excludes coverage when lien validity is questioned due to a failure to comply with consumer credit protection laws.
elements.label: list_item

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026

elements.text: Considerations (12/19/2017)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan delivery considerations, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Special Feature Codes and Pricing
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Servicing
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Special Feature Codes and Pricing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans must be identified at delivery with SFC 304. In addition, the lender must enter the following special feature codes at loan delivery:
elements.label: text

elements.text: SFC 003 for each Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan that is classified as a cash-out refinance under Fannie Mae's policy, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: SFC 007 for each Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan that is classified as a limited cash-out refinance under Fannie Mae's policy.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the lender determines that a loan secured by a mortgage on a homestead property in Texas is classified as a cash-out refinance per this Guide but is not a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan, then the loan should be delivered as a standard (non-Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan) cash-out refinance transaction and should not be identified with SFC 304.
elements.label: text

elements.text: At delivery, all Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans that are classified as cash-out refinance transactions are subject to the loan-level price adjustments applicable to cash-out refinance loans per the Lender Contract.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Servicing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender that delivers a Texas Section 50(a)(6) loan to Fannie Mae may either service the loan, enter into a subservicing arrangement with another lender, or assign the servicing concurrent with its delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Except as otherwise noted in the Selling Guide or Servicing Guide , standard Fannie Mae servicing requirements apply to Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B5-4.2-01, Native American Conventional Lending Initiative (NACLI) (06/26/2012)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on Native American Conventional Lending Initiative (NACLI), including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Eligibility
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Special Feature Codes
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: As part of Fannie Mae's commitment to offering conventional loan products to address special housing needs of the underserved, Fannie Mae developed its set of Native American conventional Housing Initiatives.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Through these initiatives, Fannie Mae purchases conventional mortgages that are made to Native Americans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Eligibility
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Any lender that is interested in participating in NACLI must obtain separate approval from Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Upon approval, the lender will obtain the applicable set of terms and conditions that may vary for the specific tribal community.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Tribes that have jurisdiction over lands restricted to tribal members are eligible.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Before any lending may take place, a tribe's ordinances must be reviewed to ensure that there is appropriate support for mortgage lending. This includes Fannie Mae's confirmation that the tribe has appropriate ordinances involving such issues as the recording of mortgages, resale, lien priority, foreclosure, and eviction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Special Feature Codes
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender must report SFC 221 for a mortgage originated under NACLI when it delivers a mortgage originated under Fannie Mae's Native American Housing Initiatives.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In addition, the lender should report all other applicable special feature codes that are needed to describe other special mortgage characteristics.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Disaster-Related Limited Cash-Out Refinance Flexibilities Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae provides flexibilities to standard limited cash-out refinance policies for borrowers who have been impacted by a natural disaster. These guidelines:
elements.label: text

elements.text: permit the refinance of non-purchase money subordinate loans obtained to finance disaster-related property repairs, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provide for a higher cash-out amount to reimburse borrowers for documented out-of-pocket expenses related to disaster-related property repairs.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: This topic outlines the specific eligibility requirements for these additional flexibilities.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Location of Property
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: These flexibilities may be applied to loans on properties located in any counties, cities, or parishes that are designated by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) as eligible for Individual Assistance as a result of a natural disaster (these areas are referred to as 'FEMA Disaster Areas').
elements.label: text

elements.text: Occupancy Status
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: These guidelines are applicable only to loans secured by the borrower's principal residence, and may not be used in connection with second homes or investment properties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Transaction Types
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A borrower may obtain:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a limited cash-out refinance to consolidate non-purchase money subordinate financing used for repair of disaster-related property damage to their principal residence. To be eligible, the subordinate financing, including any draws on an existing HELOC, must post-date the disaster. However, the borrower may pay off the entire HELOC through the limited cash-out refinance, provided that a portion of the amount was used for disaster-related expenses to repair property damage to the principal residence.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: cash-out for reimbursement of documented out-of-pocket expenses for the completed repair of disasterrelated property damage to their principal residence in an amount not to exceed the lesser of 10% of the balance of the new refinance loan or $15,000.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: All existing guidelines and requirements for limited cash-out refinance transactions listed in this section continue to apply, including those for Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans (see B5-4.1-01, Texas Section 50(a)(6) Loans).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation of Eligible Disaster-Related Expenses and Financing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must document that the subordinate financing (or a portion of the HELOC) or the entire requested cash-out amount represents funds used for completed disaster-related property repairs.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Generally, documentation includes copies of receipts, work orders, canceled checks, etc., related to the cost of materials and labor.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The borrower may not receive any reimbursement for amounts representing their sweat equity in connection with the repairs.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : All documentation must post-date the disaster and be directly related to completed repairs of damage to the property resulting from the disaster.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Limited Cash-Out Refinance DU Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Certain messages on the DU Underwriting Findings Report will not apply to loans originated under the disasterrelated limited cash-out refinance requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the loan complies with the requirements of this section, lenders may disregard the following messages:
elements.label: text

elements.text: This case is ineligible because the amount of cash taken out of the subject property equity exceeds the limit of the greater of 1% of the loan amount or $2,000 for limited cash-out refinances.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If any subordinate lien that was not used to acquire the subject property is to be paid off with first mortgage proceeds, the loan is ineligible as a limited cash-out refinance. The loan must be resubmitted as a cash-out refinance.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If subordinate liens are being paid off with the first mortgage proceeds, obtain written documentation that the subordinate lien was used to acquire the subject property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae will grant the lender the limited waiver of underwriting representations and warranties for these loans, including those mortgages that receive an Approve/Ineligible recommendation, provided the loan meets the requirements contained in this section as well as those contained in A2-2-04, Limited Waiver and Enforcement Relief of Representations and Warranties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Appraisal Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The appraisal for the property must follow standard requirements contained in Chapter B4-1, Appraisal Guidelines.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Those guidelines allow an appraisal to be based on the 'as is' condition of the property provided there are no conditions that affect the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of the property. If those conditions do exist, the property must be appraised subject to completion of the specific alterations or repairs ('as repaired') and a completion report must be obtained from the appraiser prior to delivery of the mortgage to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Delivery
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Loans originated in accordance with this section must be delivered to Fannie Mae no later than two years from the date of the disaster declaration by FEMA.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Special Feature Codes
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Loans delivered under these guidelines must include SFC 416 as part of the delivery data.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement.

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction

elements.text: Eligibility Exceptions for HomePath Properties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A HomePath property is a property that was owned and sold by Fannie Mae through a transaction resulting in the disposition of its real estate owned (REO). When the property secured by the mortgage is a HomePath property, Fannie Mae will allow certain exceptions to standard Selling Guide eligibility policies as described below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Interested Party Contributions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: In cases where the subject property is a HomePath property, an exception to the maximum financing concessions for principal residences is permitted, as described in the table below. All other requirements related to IPCs, as described in B3-4.1-02, Interested Party Contributions (IPCs), continue to apply.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Occupancy Type
elements.label: text

elements.text: LTV/CLTV Ratio
elements.label: text

elements.text: Maximum Financing
elements.label: text

elements.text: Concessions
elements.label: text

elements.text: Principal Residence
elements.label: text

elements.text: Greater than 90%
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: DU will issue a message if the amount of the IPC appears to exceed the standard financing concessions described in B3-4.1-02, Interested Party Contributions (IPCs). (DU does not apply the higher limit even if DU identifies the property as a HomePath property.) The lender must determine whether the subject transaction is a purchase of a HomePath property eligible for the higher financing concessions and document the loan file accordingly.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Certain Resale Restrictions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Notwithstanding any other provision of this Selling Guide , loans subject to resale restrictions imposed by Fannie Mae as the seller of its REO property are eligible.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Special Feature Code for HomePath Properties using the IPC Exception
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must use SFC 679 when delivering a loan secured by a HomePath property if the IPC exception applies to the transaction. This code is in addition to any other special feature codes that may apply. SFC 679 is not required for a loan secured by a HomePath property that is subject solely to the resale restriction exception.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loan-level Price Adjustment Credit for Appraisals
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender will receive a $500 loan-level price adjustment credit when Fannie Mae purchases a loan secured by a HomePath property. The following requirements apply:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The loan must be a purchase transaction for a principal residence underwritten with DU.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must obtain an appraisal for the transaction and pass the entire credit to the borrower to cover the cost of the appraisal.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must confirm the seller of the property is Fannie Mae (even though DU may identify the property as a HomePath property).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The loan must be delivered with SFC 871.

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A Community Seconds loan is subordinate financing that is originated under an affordable housing program. Fannie Mae does not purchase Community Seconds loans; however, first mortgages that are originated with this type of subordinate financing are eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae in accordance with the requirements of this Guide.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Review of Community Seconds Programs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender is responsible for reviewing Community Seconds programs to ensure the programs comply with this Guide. The Community Seconds Checklist includes a checklist that lenders may use to evaluate key considerations in determining whether to grant approval of a Community Seconds program.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table provides lender requirements for reviewing a Community Seconds program.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must...
elements.label: text

elements.text: Review all documents applicable to the program, including the legal documents (such as the
elements.label: text

elements.text: promissory note and the security instrument), the program description, and any other pertinent
elements.label: text

elements.text: documents to confirm compliance with
elements.label: text

elements.text: Section B5-5.1, Community Seconds
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determine the mortgage for the Community Seconds loan is clearly subordinate to the first mortgage. The title insurance in effect must ensure priority of the first mortgage being delivered to Fannie Mae by showing the Community Seconds loan in a subordinate position.

elements.text: Note: If a Community Seconds provider assumes the first mortgage and cures all outstanding defaults under that mortgage, the Community Seconds financing may be maintained.

elements.text: If applicable, evaluate any recorded deed restrictions or option agreements, or local ordinances that impose similar restrictions, to confirm compliance with other Fannie Mae policies, such as those applicable to resale restrictions (see Section B5-5.2, Loans with Resale Restrictions and Section B5-5.3, Shared Equity Transactions ).

elements.text: Eligible Community Seconds Providers
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: A Community Seconds loan must only be funded by one of the following entities:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a federal agency, state, county, or similar political subdivision of a state;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any city, town, village, or borough of a state that
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: has a local government and that has been created by a special legislative act,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: has been otherwise individually incorporated or chartered pursuant to state law, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: is recognized as such under the constitution or by the laws of the state in which it is located;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a housing finance agency as defined in 24 C.F.R. §266.5;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a nonprofit organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a regional Federal Home Loan Bank under one of its affordable housing programs;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an employer where a borrower is an employee (see  B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a lender, only in connection with an employer-guaranteed Community Seconds loan as part of its affordable housing program; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an Indian tribe on the most current list published by the Secretary of the Interior pursuant to 25 U.S.C. §5131.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: A corporation or other legal entity created by or owned in whole or in part by such an Indian tribe is not eligible unless it qualifies as a Tribally Designated Housing Entity, as defined in 25 U.S.C. §4103(22).
elements.label: text

elements.text: A Community Seconds provider must not be the property seller or other interested party in the transaction, except when they are the provider of a shared equity program or sweat equity program. See  B5-5.3-03, Shared Equity Transactions: Eligibility, Underwriting and Collateral Requirements  and B5-6-02, HomeReady Mortgage Underwriting Methods and Requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Uses
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A Community Seconds loan must be used to fund one or more of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: all or part of the down payment, provided the Community Seconds loan is not funded in any way through the first mortgage, such as premium pricing;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: closing costs;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: renovations to the property (including energy-related improvements); or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a permanent interest rate buydown.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In some cases, a Community Seconds loan may not involve the advancement of funds, but instead may facilitate affordability through a subsidized sales price and/or requirements related to the future sale of the home. The eligible provider may secure a Community Seconds loan against the property representing the difference between the market value and reduced sales price accepted by the seller (referred to as the subsidy). Typically, the subordinate loan has deferred payments and may be forgiven. The subordinate loan may also enforce requirements that prevent the borrower from selling the property outside of the confines of the program. The terms of the loan may not, however, restrict the sale of the property upon foreclosure or acceptance of a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a subsidy is provided, the lender may use the Affordable LTV calculation described in  B5-5.3-03, Shared Equity Transactions: Eligibility, Underwriting and Collateral Requirements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When a subsidy is provided and the property has resale restrictions that limit both income eligibility and impose a maximum resale price, the provisions in Section B5-5.3, Shared Equity Transactions also apply.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Repayment Structure
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Repayment of the Community Seconds loan may be structured in any number of ways provided the terms are consistent with those Fannie Mae considers acceptable. This includes:
elements.label: text

elements.text: requiring fully amortizing, equal monthly payments;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: deferring payments for some period before changing to fully amortizing, equal monthly payments;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: deferring payments over the entire term, unless the loan is paid off or the property is sold before the maturity date of the loan; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: forgiving debt over time.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When the borrower's employer is the provider of the Community Seconds loan, the financing terms may provide for the employer to require full repayment of the debt if the borrower's employment is terminated (either voluntarily or involuntarily, for reasons other than those related to disability) before the maturity date of the Community Seconds loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When repayment is required, the maturity date of the Community Seconds loan, or the due date of any balloon payment on the Community Seconds loan, cannot be before the earlier of:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 15 years after the note date of the first mortgage, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the maturity date of the first mortgage.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When repayment of the Community Seconds loan is deferred for five years or more, a lender is not required to include a monthly payment for the Community Seconds loan in its calculation of the borrower's debt-to-income ratio. When repayment is deferred for fewer than five years, the lender must include the monthly payment amount that will be required after the end of the deferral period in its calculation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The Community Seconds loan must be a fixed-rate loan and the interest rate may not be more than 2% (200 basis points) higher than the initial note rate of the first mortgage.

elements.text: B5-5.1-03, Community Seconds: Shared Appreciation Transactions (11/01/2023)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on shared appreciation transactions, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Provider Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligibility
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Repayment Distribution Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Delivery Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Shared appreciation programs are a type of Community Seconds offering that create affordability for eligible borrowers by providing down payment, closing cost assistance, and/or funding for renovations to the property, including energy-related improvements, in exchange for repayment of an interest-free loan and a share in any future appreciation to the property. The program provider receives the original amount advanced and a predetermined percentage of any appreciation in value upon the occurrence of specified events defined in the program's legal documentation. Events may include the subsequent sale (or other transfer) of the home and be dependent on the availability of funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Appreciation in value is defined as the positive difference between the original sales price of the property and the subsequent value of the property determined in accordance with the shared appreciation program's legal documents (which may but is not required to take into account amounts paid by the borrower to improve the property with the knowledge or consent of the shared appreciation program).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: This topic pertains only to shared appreciation loans. For information on shared equity transactions, see Section B5-5.3, Shared Equity Transactions .
elements.label: text

elements.text: Provider Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The provider of a shared appreciation loan must
elements.label: text

elements.text: be an eligible Community Seconds provider,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: be responsible for the administration and oversight of the program, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: advance its own funds (or those of other parties for whom it is administering the program, so long as each such party is an eligible Community Seconds provider) to the borrower.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: The lender of the first mortgage may advance the funds for the shared appreciation loan if within six months of loan closing the lender assigns the loan to a housing finance agency as defined in 23 C.F.R. §266.5. In this scenario, the housing finance agency will be considered an acceptable Community Seconds shared appreciation provider.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Shared appreciation loans are only eligible as Community Seconds loans. Fannie Mae will purchase first mortgages that are originated with this type of subordinate financing, but the first mortgage must be a purchase or limited cash-out refinance transaction and otherwise meet the requirements of this Guide.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table provides additional eligibility requirements for shared appreciation loans.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: The shared appreciation loan must comply with the requirements in  B5-5.1-01, Community Seconds Loans  and  B5-5.1-02,
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Expenses or fees must not be imposed on the borrower after loan origination, except in cases of default or in connection
elements.label: text

elements.text: with borrower-initiated transactions, as defined in the program's legal documentation.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: appreciation in value, the borrower must not have any further obligation to the shared appreciation provider or any
elements.label: text

elements.text: assignee.

elements.text: explicitly indicated as permitted in the program's legal documents, a current value
elements.label: text

elements.text: established by a third-party, commercial automated valuation model (AVM).
elements.label: text

elements.text: For limited cash-out refinances, appreciation in value may be based on any method
elements.label: text

elements.text: included in the program's legal documentation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Criteria
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: Provider's share of appreciation
elements.label: text

elements.text: Except as permitted below, the provider's share of appreciation must not exceed the
elements.label: text

elements.text: "Standard Percentage" which is derived by dividing the original shared appreciation loan
elements.label: text

elements.text: amount by the original sales price.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For example, if the provider contributed a 10% down payment towards the purchase of a
elements.label: text

elements.text: home, the provider cannot share in appreciation greater than 10% (the Standard
elements.label: text

elements.text: Percentage) when the home is sold.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions
elements.label: text

elements.text: The provider's share of appreciation may be greater than the Standard Percentage in
elements.label: text

elements.text: either of the two scenarios below:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 1. The borrower must first recover the following (which will result in a reduction in the
elements.label: text

elements.text: amount of appreciation) before the provider is able to share in appreciation:
elements.label: text

elements.text: • any portion of the down payment that came from the borrower's own funds,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • reasonable costs of selling the property (such as a sales commission),
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the costs of any improvements made by the borrower to the property (provided they
elements.label: text

elements.text: were allowed under the program's legal documentation and not otherwise taken into
elements.label: text

elements.text: account in determining the amount of appreciation), and
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the principal portion of all payments the borrower made on the first mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 2. The provider may share in appreciation greater than the Standard Percentage if
elements.label: text

elements.text: • in the first year following origination of the shared appreciation loan, the share does not
elements.label: text

elements.text: initially exceed 75%, or
elements.label: text

elements.text: • after the first year following origination of the shared appreciation loan, the share is
elements.label: text

elements.text: reduced at least proportionally each year, so the share is equal to or less than the
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standard Percentage no later than five years following origination of the shared
elements.label: text

elements.text: appreciation loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For example, if the provider's share of appreciation is 70% in the first year, and the
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standard Percentage is 10%, then the provider's share must decrease by at least 15%
elements.label: text

elements.text: annually (70% minus 10%, divided by 4), so that in the second, third, and fourth years, the
elements.label: text

elements.text: provider's share of appreciation must not exceed 55%, 40%, and 25%, respectively.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Delivery Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must report SFC 176 when delivering a loan with shared appreciation.
